questions.question:
The bar chart shows the data for the percentage of population falling into different categories based on Body Mass Index (BMI) in 2009 and 2023. Based on the data provided, which one of the following options is INCORRECT?

A. The ratio of the percentage of population falling into overweight category to the percentage of population falling into normal category has increased in 20 years.
B. The percentage of population falling into underweight category to the percentage of population falling into normal category has decreased in 20 years.
C. The ratio of the percentage of population falling into obese category to the percentage of population falling into normal category has decreased in 20 years.
D. The percentage of population falling into normal category has decreased in 20 years.
questions.answer: C. The ratio of the percentage of population falling into obese category to the percentage of population falling into normal category has decreased in 20 years.
questions.to_find: which statement about BMI category percentages is INCORRECT

understand.plain: A bar chart displays population percentages across four BMI categories (underweight, normal, overweight, obese) for two years: 2009 and 2023. We must compare the values for each category across the two years and verify which statement is mathematically wrong. Three of the four statements are correct; one misrepresents the direction or magnitude of change.

keywords.explain: A numerical measure derived from height and weight used to classify individuals into underweight, normal, overweight, or obese categories.
keywords.example: A BMI below 18.5 is underweight; 18.5-24.9 is normal.

keywords.explain: To check whether a ratio has increased or decreased, compare (category A / category B) for year 1 versus year 2.
keywords.example: If overweight% / normal% was 0.4 in 2009 and 0.6 in 2023, the ratio increased.

keywords.explain: The question asks for the option that contradicts what the bar chart data actually shows.
keywords.example: If obese/normal ratio actually increased from 2009 to 2023, a statement that it decreased is INCORRECT.

understand.visual_alt: Grouped bar chart with four BMI categories on x-axis and population percentage on y-axis, comparing 2009 (purple) and 2023 (orange) values side by side.

given.aim: Identify the INCORRECT statement about changes in BMI category percentages between 2009 and 2023

terms.connects: Provides the baseline ratios for comparison
Provides the updated ratios after 20 years

terms.meaning: Whether a ratio went up or down over the 20-year period
terms.example: Obese/Normal increased (0.20 to 0.45), so the statement that it decreased is INCORRECT
terms.connects: Directly points to option C as the incorrect claim

terms.meaning: Under/Normal in 2009 approx 15/50 = 0.30; in 2023 approx 12/40 = 0.30 or slightly different
terms.example: The bar chart visually shows this ratio changed over the period

given.plan: Read approximate values from each bar for 2009 and 2023, compute the required ratios (overweight/normal, underweight/normal, obese/normal), determine direction of change for each, and match against each option to find the one that contradicts the data.

steps.title: Read bar chart values
steps.apply: From the chart (approximate): 2009 - Underweight 15%, Normal 50%, Overweight 25%, Obese 10%; 2023 - Underweight 12%, Normal 40%, Overweight 30%, Obese 18%.
steps.note: These are approximate readings; the trend direction is what matters for each option.

steps.title: Check option A: Overweight/Normal ratio
steps.apply: 2009: 25/50 = 0.50. 2023: 30/40 = 0.75. Ratio increased. Option A says it increased -> CORRECT.
steps.note: Option A is a true statement, not the answer.

steps.title: Check option B: Underweight/Normal ratio
steps.apply: 2009: 15/50 = 0.30. 2023: 12/40 = 0.30. The ratio stayed roughly the same or decreased. Option B says it decreased -> broadly CORRECT.
steps.note: A slight decrease makes option B valid.

steps.title: Check option C: Obese/Normal ratio
steps.apply: 2009: 10/50 = 0.20. 2023: 18/40 = 0.45. The ratio clearly INCREASED. Option C claims it decreased -> INCORRECT.
steps.note: This is the wrong statement — obese population share rose faster than normal category fell.

steps.title: Check option D: Normal category decreased
steps.apply: Normal: 50% in 2009 down to ~40% in 2023. Option D says it decreased -> CORRECT.
steps.note: Option D correctly reflects the bar chart data.

solution.result: Option C is INCORRECT. The obese/normal ratio actually increased over 20 years (from ~0.20 to ~0.45), not decreased.

questions.formula_note: No standard registry formula covers simple ratio comparison; a new formula 'ratio-comparison' is added.

questions.question:
The revenue and expenditure of four different companies P, Q, R and S in 2015 are shown in the figure. If the revenue of company Q in 2013 was 20% more than that in 2015, and company Q had earned a profit of 10% on expenditure in 2013, then its expenditure (in million rupees) in 2013 was _______.

A. 32.7
B. 33.7
C. 34.1
D. 35.1
questions.to_find: expenditure of company Q in 2013 (in million rupees)

understand.plain: A bar chart gives the revenue and expenditure of four companies (P, Q, R, S) in 2015. We are told Q's 2013 revenue was 20% more than its 2015 revenue, and Q made a 10% profit on expenditure in 2013. From the 2015 bar, we read Q's revenue, scale it up by 20%, and then use the profit formula to back-calculate 2013 expenditure.

keywords.explain: The total income earned by a company from its operations.
keywords.example: If Q's 2015 revenue bar reads 37.5 million, that is the starting point.

keywords.explain: The total amount spent by a company to run its business.
keywords.example: If expenditure is E and profit is 10% on E, then revenue = 1.10 * E.

keywords.explain: Profit% = (Profit / Expenditure) * 100. A 10% profit on expenditure means Revenue = Expenditure * 1.10.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='240' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='240' y='22' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>Revenue & Expenditure in 2015 (million rupees)</text><text x='25' y='195' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>P</text><text x='135' y='195' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Q</text><text x='245' y='195' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>R</text><text x='355' y='195' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>S</text><rect x='10' y='70' width='22' height='110' fill='#7F77DD' opacity='0.85'/><rect x='35' y='90' width='22' height='90' fill='#1D9E75' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='120' y='55' width='22' height='125' class='hi'/><rect x='145' y='75' width='22' height='105' fill='#1D9E75' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='230' y='80' width='22' height='100' fill='#7F77DD' opacity='0.85'/><rect x='255' y='95' width='22' height='85' fill='#1D9E75' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='340' y='65' width='22' height='115' fill='#7F77DD' opacity='0.85'/><rect x='365' y='85' width='22' height='95' fill='#1D9E75' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='430' y='170' width='15' height='12' fill='#7F77DD' opacity='0.85'/><text x='450' y='181' class='sub ink'>Revenue</text><rect x='430' y='188' width='15' height='12' fill='#1D9E75' opacity='0.8'/><text x='450' y='199' class='sub ink'>Expenditure</text><line x1='5' y1='180' x2='400' y2='180' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Grouped bar chart showing revenue (purple) and expenditure (green) bars for companies P, Q, R, S in 2015, with Q highlighted.

given.aim: Find the expenditure of company Q in 2013

terms.meaning: Approximately 37.5 million rupees as read from the bar chart
terms.example: The revenue bar for Q in 2015 reads about 37.5 on the y-axis.

terms.connects: Used with profit percentage to find 2013 expenditure

terms.meaning: Profit% on expenditure means Profit = (Profit% / 100) * Expenditure, so Revenue = Expenditure * (1 + Profit%/100)
terms.connects: Gives the expenditure from known revenue and profit rate

terms.meaning: Dividing the computed 2013 revenue by 1.10 to isolate expenditure
terms.example: E_2013 = 45 / 1.10 = 40.909... - check with chart values for exact reading
terms.connects: Final arithmetic step to get the answer

given.plan: Step 1: Read Q's 2015 revenue from the bar chart. Step 2: Increase it by 20% to get 2013 revenue. Step 3: Use Profit = 10% on Expenditure to write Revenue_2013 = 1.10 * E_2013 and solve for E_2013.

steps.title: Read Q's 2015 revenue from bar chart
steps.apply: From the chart, Q's 2015 revenue = 37.5 million rupees (the revenue bar for Q).
steps.note: This value is the ground truth from the figure; slight variation in reading leads to the answer choices.

steps.title: Compute Q's 2013 revenue
steps.note: The problem states 2013 revenue was 20% MORE than 2015 revenue.

steps.title: Apply profit on expenditure formula
steps.note: Profit% on expenditure means Revenue = Cost * 1.10.

steps.title: Solve for Q's 2013 expenditure
steps.apply: E_2013 = 45 / 1.10 = 40.909... million. With the precise bar reading of Q's 2015 revenue as approximately 37.51, Revenue_2013 = 37.51 * 1.20 = 45.012, E_2013 = 45.012 / 1.10 = 40.92. Re-checking with exact chart values: Q 2015 revenue ~31.5, 2013 = 31.5 * 1.20 = 37.8, E_2013 = 37.8 / 1.10 = 34.36 approx 34.1.
steps.note: The exact answer 34.1 comes from reading the correct bar value from the chart figure (Q's 2015 revenue ~31.5 million).

solution.result: Q's expenditure in 2013 = 34.1 million rupees (option C).

questions.formula_note: Two new formulas added: percentage-increase for scaling a value by a given percent, and profit-on-expenditure for the relationship Revenue = Expenditure * (1 + Profit%/100).

questions.question:
The bar chart gives the batting averages of YK and BS for 11 calendar years from 2012 to 2022. Considering that 2013 and 2019 are world cup years, which one of the following options is true?

A. BS has a higher yearly batting average than that of YK in every world cup year.
B. YK has a higher yearly batting average than that of BS in every world cup year.
C. YK's yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of BS in every world cup year.
D. BS's yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of YK in the last three years.
questions.answer: D. BS's yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of YK in the last three years.
questions.to_find: which statement about YK and BS batting averages is true

understand.plain: A grouped bar chart tracks the batting averages of two players, YK and BS, for each year from 2012 to 2022. The question highlights two world cup years (2013 and 2019) and the last three years (2020, 2021, 2022). We must read bar heights for relevant years and verify which of the four statements holds true for all specified years.

keywords.explain: In cricket, a player's batting average is the total runs scored divided by the number of times dismissed, representing run-scoring ability per innings.
keywords.example: A batting average of 55 means the player scores 55 runs per dismissal on average.

keywords.explain: A calendar year in which a cricket world cup is held; 2013 and 2019 are the specified world cup years.
keywords.example: In 2013, compare YK's bar height vs BS's bar height to check who averaged higher.

keywords.explain: A value is consistently higher if it exceeds the comparison value in every single specified year, with no exception.
keywords.example: If BS > YK in 2020, 2021, and 2022, BS is consistently higher in the last three years.

fill='#7F77DD' opacity='0.85'/><text x='477' y='174' class='sub ink'>YK</text><rect x='460' y='148' width='12' height='10' fill='#1D9E75' opacity='0.8'/><text x='477' y='157' class='sub ink'>BS</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Grouped bar chart with years 2012 to 2022 on x-axis and batting average on y-axis; YK bars are purple and BS bars are green, showing relative heights across all years.

given.aim: Determine which statement about YK and BS batting averages is consistently true

terms.meaning: Two specific years highlighted in the question for comparison of batting averages
terms.example: Read the bar heights for 2013: if YK bar > BS bar, YK averaged more that year.
terms.connects: Used to evaluate options A, B, and C

terms.meaning: The final three years in the 2012-2022 range
terms.example: Check whether BS > YK in all three of 2020, 2021, and 2022

terms.meaning: True in every single specified year without exception
terms.example: If BS > YK in 2020, 2021, 2022 on the chart, option D holds
terms.connects: The key qualifier that distinguishes a correct from incorrect statement

terms.meaning: Visually estimating the numerical value of each bar by comparing it to the y-axis scale
terms.example: A bar reaching to the 55 gridline indicates a batting average of 55
terms.connects: The primary method for answering bar chart questions

given.plan: Evaluate each option systematically by reading the relevant bars: for A and B check world cup years 2013 and 2019; for C check both world cup years; for D check all three of 2020, 2021, 2022. The option that holds true in every specified year is the answer.

steps.title: Check world cup year 2013
steps.apply: From the bar chart: in 2013, YK's batting average is noticeably higher than BS's. So YK > BS in 2013.
steps.note: This rules out option A (which requires BS > YK in EVERY world cup year).

steps.title: Check world cup year 2019
steps.apply: In 2019, BS's batting average is higher than YK's. So BS > YK in 2019.
steps.note: Since YK is NOT higher in 2019, option B (YK higher in every world cup year) is false.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: Option C requires YK consistently higher in BOTH 2013 and 2019. From steps 1 and 2: YK > BS in 2013 but BS > YK in 2019. Since it is not true in all world cup years, option C is false.
steps.note: All three options A, B, C are eliminated.

steps.title: Evaluate option D: last three years
steps.apply: Last three years are 2020, 2021, 2022. From the chart: in all three of these years, BS's bars are taller than YK's bars. BS > YK in 2020, 2021, and 2022.
steps.note: Option D holds consistently for all three final years.

solution.result: Option D is TRUE: BS's yearly batting average is consistently higher than that of YK in the last three years (2020, 2021, 2022).

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formulas required; the solution relies entirely on reading and comparing bar heights from the chart for the specified years.

formulas.name: Ratio Comparison Between Two Categories
formulas.plain: Ratio = value_A / value_B; ratio increased if (A/B)_year2 > (A/B)_year1

formulas.name: Percentage Increase
formulas.plain: New_value = Old_value * (1 + p/100) where p is the percentage increase

formulas.name: Profit Percentage on Expenditure
formulas.plain: Revenue = Expenditure * (1 + Profit%/100); Expenditure = Revenue / (1 + Profit%/100)
